example_name: California AG Enforcement Against Sephora (CCPA Authorized Agent)
example_background: In 2022, Sephora, a major cosmetics retailer, faced action from the California Attorney General for multiple violations of the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). One key issue involved the company's handling of consumer rights requests submitted through authorized agents. Sephora did not have adequate processes to verify the authority of agents acting on behalf of consumers when requests were made to access, delete, or opt-out of the sale of personal information. The company's privacy policy outlined requirements for written permission from consumers but lacked mechanisms to consistently request and review proof from agents during request processing. This occurred amid broader CCPA enforcement efforts following the law's effective date in 2020, where Sephora processed millions of consumer requests but failed to implement robust verification protocols for third-party submissions. The incident highlighted gaps in enterprise-level privacy operations for handling agent-authorized requests at scale.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General investigated Sephora's CCPA compliance practices. Sephora agreed to pay a $1.2 million settlement and committed to implementing a comprehensive CCPA compliance program, including specific procedures for verifying authorized agents by requesting written permission and maintaining records of verifications for at least 24 months.
source_rule: Authorized Agents: To exercise your rights using an Authorized Agent (as defined under applicable law), you must provide your Authorized Agent with written permission to exercise your rights on your behalf, and we may request a copy of this written permission from your Authorized Agent when they make a request on your behalf. We reserve the right to deny a request from an Authorized Agent that does not submit proof that they have been authorized by you to act on your behalf.

example_name: DoorDash CCPA Authorized Agent Verification Settlement
example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, was scrutinized in 2022 for its data privacy practices under the CCPA. The company received numerous consumer rights requests, including those from authorized agents seeking to exercise rights like deletion and opt-out on behalf of users. DoorDash's initial processes did not systematically require or validate written authorization from consumers when agents submitted requests. This led to instances where requests were processed without proper verification of the agent's authority, potentially exposing consumer data inappropriately. The issue arose during a period of increased regulatory focus on tech companies handling large volumes of personal data from California residents, with DoorDash's user base generating high request volumes.
process_and_outcome: Following investigation by the California Attorney General, DoorDash settled for $375,000 and agreed to enhance its privacy program. This included developing policies to deny agent requests without proof of written permission and training staff on verification procedures.

example_background: In 2023, GoodRx, a prescription drug discount service, disclosed personal health information of millions of consumers to third-party advertisers like Facebook and Google without proper consent mechanisms. While not exclusively about authorized agents, the case involved mishandling of consumer requests to limit data sharing, where third parties acted without verified consumer authorization. GoodRx failed to implement processes to challenge or verify unauthorized third-party access to health data, paralleling agent verification requirements. This occurred in the context of growing scrutiny on health data privacy under FTC rules, affecting up to 55 million consumers from 2017 to 2020.
process_and_outcome: The FTC investigated and settled with GoodRx for $1.5 million in penalties, plus injunctive relief requiring implementation of agent verification-like processes for third-party data requests, including proof of authority before processing.

example_name: Equifax Data Subject Access Request Failures
example_background: In the aftermath of a major data breach affecting 147 million individuals, Equifax faced numerous consumer requests for access to their personal data under data protection laws. Many consumers submitted formal requests to understand what data was compromised and held by the company. Equifax experienced significant delays in processing these requests, often exceeding the 30-day or one-month timelines mandated by regulations. Consumers reported waiting several months without responses, and some requests were outright ignored or lost in backlog. The company cited high volume and remediation efforts as reasons for delays, but this led to complaints filed with data protection authorities. The situation highlighted challenges in scaling data subject rights handling during crisis periods, with internal systems overwhelmed and inadequate staffing for verification processes.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated complaints about Equifax's handling of data access requests. Equifax was fined £500,000 in 2018 for data protection failures including inadequate response to subject access requests. The company was required to overhaul its data rights processes and report compliance improvements. Additional scrutiny came from the US Federal Trade Commission settlement which mandated better consumer request handling.
source_rule: Appeals: If we refuse to take action on a request within a reasonable period of time after receiving your request, you may appeal our decision via https://xai-privacy.relyance.ai/. In such an appeal, you must (1) provide sufficient information to allow us to verify that you are the person about whom the original request pertains and to identify the original request, and (2) provide a description of the basis of your appeal. Please note that your appeal will be subject to your rights and obligations afforded to you under applicable law. We respond to all appeal requests as soon as we reasonably can, and no later than legally required.

example_name: Google GDPR Right of Access Enforcement
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority CNIL received multiple complaints from users who submitted data access requests to Google under GDPR. Users sought comprehensive data profiles built from their online activities. Google provided partial responses but was accused of delays beyond the one-month period and insufficient detail. The company argued technical complexities in aggregating data from vast services like Search and YouTube. Complainants appealed to CNIL after Google's internal processes failed to resolve issues satisfactorily, pointing to repeated non-responses or inadequate verifications.
process_and_outcome: CNIL investigated and issued a €50 million fine in 2019, part of which addressed failures in handling data subject rights requests promptly. Google appealed but lost; the decision reinforced strict timelines. Google enhanced its privacy dashboard to streamline future requests.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Deletion Request Failures
example_background: TikTok faced scrutiny over handling deletion requests for children's data under COPPA and GDPR. Parents and users submitted requests to remove underage accounts and associated data, but many experienced delays exceeding 30 days or 45-day extensions. Internal processes struggled with verifying parental consent and locating data across global servers. Some requests were processed partially, leaving residual data, leading to appeals filed with regulators after initial refusals or timeouts.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) fined TikTok €345 million in 2023 for child data violations, including deficient handling of deletion requests. The company was mandated to appoint a Data Protection Officer and improve rights processes. US FTC actions added separate penalties.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Appeal Process Fine
example_background: Meta (Facebook) was challenged by users exercising rights to object to data processing for behavioral advertising. After initial refusals, users appealed via Meta's mechanisms, but responses were delayed beyond legal limits and lacked sufficient justification. The cases involved thousands of requests post-Cambridge Analytica, with appeals highlighting verification hurdles and opaque decision-making on overrides.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined Meta €91 million in 2023 specifically for failing to handle data subject requests, including appeals, within mandated timelines from 2018-2020. Meta implemented automated appeal tracking systems as remediation.

example_background: Apple established a dedicated online portal for global data subject rights requests, including access, deletion, and appeals under GDPR, CCPA, and other laws. Users submit requests via privacy.apple.com, providing identity verification details. Apple processes initial requests within 30-45 days and offers an appeal form for denials or delays, requiring original request IDs and basis explanations. The company publishes transparency reports detailing request volumes and fulfillment rates, demonstrating structured handling even during high volumes.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions recorded; Apple's processes have been praised in regulatory audits. In GDPR compliance certifications and CCPA CPRA filings, Apple reported 99% on-time responses. Independent audits by third parties confirmed adherence.

Microsoft handles millions of requests annually, with automated workflows ensuring responses within legal timelines, supported by dedicated privacy teams.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's annual Transparency Reports show high compliance rates, with rare complaints upheld by regulators. EU DPC and US state AG reviews found processes exemplary, serving as industry benchmarks.

process_and_outcome: Certified compliant in multiple GDPR adequacy decisions and CCPA audits. No material fines for rights handling; used as a case study in IAPP best practices reports.

example_background: X (formerly Twitter) hosted accounts sharing child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and failed to adequately detect or report it. From 2020-2022, despite policies, the platform allowed exploitative content to proliferate, with weak age verification and moderation. Internal reports showed delays in hashing and removing known CSAM, affecting global users including minors. This came under spotlight post-Elon Musk acquisition amid broader content moderation reviews.
process_and_outcome: U.S. DOJ and FBI investigated; X reported improvements but faced a 2023 class action lawsuit. X enhanced NCMEC reporting, hired more moderators, and implemented photo DNA hashing, though litigation continues.

example_name: Google GDPR Privacy Policy Consent Fine
example_background: In 2020, the French data protection authority CNIL fined Google 50 million euros for violations related to its handling of user consent under GDPR. Google's privacy policy had undergone updates to incorporate GDPR requirements, but the authority found that the consent mechanisms bundled various data processing purposes without granular options and lacked transparency about policy changes. Users were deemed to accept changes by continued use of services like Android apps and Google Search without adequate prior notice or easy opt-out paths, affecting millions of EU users who interacted with the services post-policy publication.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued the fine following public complaints and its own investigations. Google appealed but lost subsequent court challenges. The case prompted Google to revise its consent banners and policy notification processes across EU services.

example_background: In 2021, the Irish Data Protection Commission fined WhatsApp 225 million euros, a record at the time, for transparency issues after a 2021 privacy policy update. The update aimed to clarify data sharing with parent company Meta for business purposes but was presented in a way that confused users about personal data processing changes. WhatsApp published the updated policy on its website with an effective date but relied on in-app pop-ups that did not fully explain the shifts, leading users to continue using the service under the new terms without clear understanding of expanded data uses.
process_and_outcome: The fine followed investigations by multiple EU regulators coordinated through the European Data Protection Board. WhatsApp implemented improved notification designs and faced ongoing scrutiny.

The policy clearly states changes will be posted on Apple's website with effective dates, and users are notified via in-app prompts and legal pages. For ATT, iOS users receive explicit prompts before apps can track across sites/apps, with policy updates detailing data practices. Continued use post-update applies new terms, but Apple provides tools like privacy nutrition labels and annual privacy reports to enhance user awareness.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; praised by regulators and privacy advocates. EU and U.S. regulators acknowledged improvements in transparency during GDPR compliance reviews.

example_background: In 2020-2023, the Irish DPC fined TikTok multiple times, including 345 million euros in 2023, partly for inadequate handling of children's data and transparency in policy updates. TikTok updated its privacy policy in 2020 without sufficient notice to parents or granular consent for under-16 users, allowing data processing changes that affected minors who continued using the app post-update. The policies were published online but lacked age-appropriate notifications.
process_and_outcome: Cross-EU regulatory investigation led to the fine and orders for policy revisions, age verification, and better update notifications.

example_background: Clearview AI collected billions of facial images by scraping public websites without consent, with privacy policy updates posted online stating continued service use applies changes. process_and_outcome: Multiple fines including £7.5 million from UK ICO, 20 million euros from France CNIL, and ongoing U.S. class actions. Orders to delete EU data.

example_background: In 2020, the UK ICO fined Marriott 18.4 million pounds for a data breach affecting 339 million guests, compounded by failures in privacy policy transparency after acquiring Starwood Hotels. Policy updates post-acquisition did not adequately notify users about integrated data processing changes, with users continuing to book services under new terms without clear disclosure of merged data handling.
process_and_outcome: ICO investigation post-breach revealed policy shortcomings; fine issued with remediation requirements including better update processes.

example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with personal data of up to 383 million guests, including names, passport numbers, payment card details, and travel information. A breach detected in 2018 (ongoing since 2014) exposed unencrypted passport numbers and other sensitive data due to weak access controls and failure to detect unauthorized access earlier. European guests' data was among those compromised, with issues in notifying affected individuals promptly.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in November 2020 (reduced from £99 million on appeal). Marriott enhanced data protection measures and provided credit monitoring to affected users.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Challenge in Italy
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority investigated Google's use of Google Analytics on websites of Italian organizations, focusing on data transfers to the US. Cookies collected IP addresses and user behavior data, transferred without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II, exposing data to US surveillance laws. Multiple websites were ordered to halt use until compliance.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, the Garante blocked Google Analytics transfers, citing GDPR non-compliance. Similar actions followed in Austria and France, prompting tool modifications like IP anonymization.

example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority investigated Google's processing of personal data for personalized advertising on Android devices and its search engine. The probe focused on how Google collected and used location data, advertising IDs, and behavioral data from millions of users without providing sufficient transparency about processing purposes. Google's privacy policy was deemed too general, bundling multiple purposes under vague terms, making it difficult for users to understand the legal bases applied to their data. This affected core services reaching EU users extensively.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed four fines totaling €50 million in January 2019, marking one of the first major GDPR penalties. Google appealed but lost subsequent reviews. Additional fines followed from other authorities for similar issues.

This feature provides clear information on tracking purposes and uses consent as the legal basis, with options to limit data processing. Apple's privacy nutrition labels detail data usage per app, enhancing transparency for billions of devices.
process_and_outcome: The implementation has been praised by regulators and privacy advocates. It reduced cross-app tracking significantly, with over 80% of EU users opting out, and no major enforcement actions against Apple for this practice.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Schrems II Violations
example_background: Multiple EU data protection authorities found that websites using Google Analytics transferred personal data including IP addresses, device IDs, and usage data to US servers without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II ruling, which invalidated EU-US Privacy Shield. This involved processing technical usage data and communication data for analytics purposes.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the French CNIL ordered websites to stop using Google Analytics, with Austria's DPA fining a site €100,000. Similar actions in Italy and others led to widespread reconfiguration of analytics tools.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Cookie Consent Violations
example_background: Google implemented its Analytics and advertising services on numerous websites worldwide, including in the EU, where it automatically set cookies to track user behavior across sites for analytics, personalization of search results, and ad customization. The service collected IP addresses, browser data, and timestamps without prior user consent, claiming legitimate interests in service improvement and fraud prevention. European regulators found that the pre-set cookies bypassed user choice and that Google's framework did not provide granular control over data processing for personalization purposes.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL regulator issued a 150 million euro fine in 2022 after investigating complaints. Google was directed to cease unlawful processing within two months and ensure compliance in future deployments. Multiple other EU authorities followed with similar actions.

process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a 35 million euro fine in 2020 following a complaint. Amazon was required to enhance user controls and transparency notices regarding data use for personalization.

example_name: PayPal GDPR Fine by Dutch Regulator
example_background: PayPal Payments Foundation, the European arm of PayPal, was investigated for its processing of personal data including account and payment information for over 23 million users. The company collected extensive payment data, communication logs, and account details but failed to adequately inform users about the purposes, storage periods, and transfers of this data to other parts of the PayPal group. Data was transferred internationally without sufficient safeguards, and users were not clearly notified about automated decision-making processes used in payment processing.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) imposed a €4.65 million fine in 2020 after a formal investigation. PayPal was required to revise its privacy notices and data processing practices to enhance transparency.

example_background: Stripe, a global payment processing platform, prepared for and implemented Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) requirements under PSD2 across Europe. The company processes vast amounts of account data, communication data from transactions, and payment data for merchants and customers. Stripe updated its systems to include biometric verification, two-factor authentication, and dynamic linking for payments, ensuring all processed data was handled with explicit user consent or contractual necessity while maintaining seamless transaction flows.
process_and_outcome: Stripe successfully met PSD2 deadlines without major disruptions or fines, earning recognition for its proactive compliance efforts. The implementation reduced fraud rates and supported continued payment processing operations.

The company processes millions of payments daily, including account numbers, transaction communications, and payment details, using tokenization, encryption, and segmented networks to protect data throughout the payment lifecycle without storing sensitive authentication data post-authorization.
process_and_outcome: Square has consistently achieved PCI DSS Level 1 Service Provider status, audited by qualified security assessors, avoiding fines and supporting secure payment processing for merchants.

example_background: In 2018, Ticketmaster, part of Live Nation Entertainment, experienced unauthorized access to its systems via a third-party chat service provider, Inbenta. Attackers obtained personal data including names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and partial payment card details of approximately 560 million users worldwide. The breached systems handled ticket purchase payments, account registrations, and related communications.
process_and_outcome: The UK's ICO investigated and fined Ticketmaster £1.25 million in 2020. The company improved third-party risk management and data access controls following the incident.
source_rule: To process payments for our ServiceWe process Account Data, Communication Data, and Payment Data This is necessary to perform our contract with you, or - if there is no contract - this is necessary for our legitimate interest in processing a payment at your request
relevant_rules: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS)

The company integrated 3D Secure protocols and exemption handling for low-risk transactions, ensuring all data processing aligned with contractual obligations for merchants and legitimate interests in facilitating payments.
process_and_outcome: Adyen achieved compliance ahead of regulatory deadlines, with no enforcement actions, and reported lower fraud rates post-implementation.

example_background: OpenAI's ChatGPT service processed user prompts and generated responses using vast datasets including web-scraped content and user interactions to train and refine its language models. Italian authorities raised concerns after users reported personal data from years prior appearing in chatbot outputs, stemming from training on publicly available internet data that inadvertently included sensitive information. The service was temporarily blocked in Italy pending compliance measures, highlighting issues with processing user content and public data for model improvement without explicit consents.
process_and_outcome: Italy's data protection authority issued an order to halt processing of Italian users' data, prompting OpenAI to implement age verification, delete memory functions for users, and provide transparency reports. No fine was imposed initially, but ongoing monitoring was required.

process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a 50 million euro fine in 2019, the first under GDPR for consent violations. Google appealed but implemented changes to consent banners.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Schrems II Invalidations
example_background: Google used data from EU websites via Analytics and Ad Manager tools, transferring it to US servers for processing and model improvement in advertising and analytics services. Transfers relied on Privacy Shield, which was invalidated. Post-Schrems II, Google continued using standard contractual clauses but faced challenges over US surveillance laws impacting legitimate interests balancing tests for data exports used in AI and performance enhancements.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL banned transfers in 2022, upheld by other EU regulators. Google suspended transfers for some services, implemented EU data residency options, and faced class actions. CNIL issued formal orders with ongoing compliance monitoring.
source_rule: This is necessary for our legitimate interests in improving the accuracy and performance of our models used to power the Services and to train more advanced models

The company performs detailed LIAs documenting necessity for business operations, user benefits, and safeguards like pseudonymization and objection rights exercised by millions annually.

example_background: In 2018, WhatsApp, owned by Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook), updated its terms to allow sharing user data with other Facebook companies for advertising purposes. This affected over 1 billion European users, processing data such as phone numbers and metadata for commercial interests without sufficient prior consent or balancing against user expectations. Spanish users challenged the changes, leading to scrutiny of whether the data sharing was necessary and proportionate given privacy impacts.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) imposed a €225 million fine in September 2021 following an investigation. WhatsApp appealed but ultimately paid the fine and adjusted its data practices in the EU.
source_rule: When we process your personal information because this is necessary for our, or someone else’s legitimate interests, we have carried out a balancing test to document our interests, to consider what the impact of the processing is on individuals, and to determine if those interests outweigh our interests in the processing taking place. You can find out more information about this balancing test by using the contact details at the end of this notice.

process_and_outcome: The AEPD fined Google €10 million in December 2022. Similar actions were taken against Meta, and both companies modified their services for EU compliance.

The investigation focused on deactivation of user accounts, processing of reports, and data retention for judicial proceedings, questioning alignment with legitimate interests in limiting damages and pursuing remedies.

example_name: Microsoft Zero Trust Security Model Implementation
example_background: Microsoft, a leading technology company, adopted and promoted a Zero Trust security architecture across its cloud services and internal IT infrastructure. This model assumes no implicit trust and requires continuous verification for all users, devices, and applications accessing resources. Microsoft processes vast amounts of account data, technical usage data, and communication data through Azure, Office 365, and other platforms. The implementation includes multi-factor authentication, just-in-time access, and real-time threat detection using AI-driven tools like Microsoft Defender, ensuring ongoing monitoring to detect unauthorized activities and maintain infrastructure security.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's approach has been recognized in industry reports for reducing breach incidents and improving response times. No major regulatory fines related to security failures in recent years, and the company publishes transparency reports showing proactive threat mitigations.
source_rule: To ensure the security and integrity of our ServiceWe process Account Data, Communication Data, User Content, Feedback Data and Technical Usage Data This is necessary for our and others’ legitimate interests (to detect or prevent activities which are illegal or breach our Terms of Service or Authorized Use Policy, and/or to manage the security of our IT infrastructure, and the safety and security of our employees, customers and vendors)
involved_parties: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)

Its platform uses adaptive multi-factor authentication, API security, and threat analytics to monitor and prevent unauthorized access. Following its own 2022 support system breach, Okta invested in device trust, behavioral analytics, and zero trust principles to secure its infrastructure, ensuring continuous detection of suspicious activities across customer environments.
process_and_outcome: Post-incident improvements led to no repeat major breaches, with Okta achieving SOC 2 Type II compliance and positive customer security audits. The company regularly publishes security whitepapers and transparency reports.

In response to ongoing threats, it deploys global DDoS mitigation, Web Application Firewalls (WAF), and automated threat blocking powered by machine learning. Following a 2019 unencrypted logs exposure, Cloudflare implemented ephemeral logging and zero-log policies where feasible, ensuring infrastructure security through real-time analytics and rapid incident response capabilities.
process_and_outcome: Cloudflare maintains ISO 27001 certification and publishes detailed incident reports, demonstrating effective threat management without regulatory penalties. Its services have blocked trillions of threats annually.

example_background: Following GDPR enforcement, Google updated its Android and web privacy controls in 2019-2020 to provide granular consent for sensitive data processing. Users can now manage permissions for location, camera, microphone, and personalized ads, with options to limit data linked to sensitive categories like health searches or political interests inferred from behavior. The changes included a unified privacy hub and automatic deletion of location/activity data after set periods. This was in response to prior fines and to standardize practices across services like YouTube, Search, and Maps, ensuring no collection of explicitly sensitive data without consent and transparency on inferences.
process_and_outcome: Google avoided further major GDPR fines post-implementation, with the Irish DPC closing several probes favorably. The company reported billions of privacy settings managed annually, enhancing user trust and compliance.
source_rule: We do not aim to collect sensitive personal information (e.g., information related to racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion or other beliefs, health, biometric scans, criminal background, or trade union membership) and ask that you do NOT provide us with any such information.

example_background: Microsoft established 'Responsible AI Principles' in 2019, including fairness, reliability, privacy, and inclusiveness, specifically addressing avoidance of bias related to race, religion, or other sensitive attributes in AI systems. For products like Azure AI and facial recognition, Microsoft implemented impact assessments, data minimization for sensitive categories, and transparency reports on training data exclusions. The company also introduced tools like Content Moderator to flag sensitive content without storing it, aligning with internal policies prohibiting collection of special category data unless strictly necessary and consented.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft published annual AI transparency reports and avoided penalties, positioning as a leader in ethical AI. Partnerships with regulators reinforced compliance.
source_rule: We do not aim to collect sensitive personal information (e.g., information related to racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion or other beliefs, health, biometric scans, criminal background, or trade union membership) and ask that you do NOT provide us with any such information.

example_background: In 2022, ride-hailing company Didi Global was penalized by Chinese regulators for illegally collecting and sharing user personal data with foreign service providers. Didi stored sensitive data including phone numbers, emails, and location information on U.S.-based cloud servers operated by third-party providers without proper approvals. The company expanded aggressively using data analytics firms and cloud services for processing vast amounts of personal information from hundreds of millions of users, bypassing required security assessments for cross-border data transfers.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) ordered Didi to conduct a cybersecurity review, imposed a fine of RMB 1.2 billion (about $176 million), and required the company to cease its app from app stores. Didi also faced a $1.3 billion IPO-related fine from the China Securities Regulatory Commission.

example_name: Schrems II and EU-US Data Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy advocate, filed complaints against Facebook Ireland for transferring personal data of European users to servers in the United States. The case highlighted concerns over mass surveillance practices under US laws, leading to examination of the adequacy of data protection frameworks for transatlantic transfers. The Court of Justice of the European Union conducted a detailed review of the Privacy Shield framework, which had been established to facilitate data flows between the EU and US following the invalidation of the Safe Harbor agreement in 2015. The proceedings involved analysis of national security access to data stored in the US and the level of protection afforded to EU data subjects.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the CJEU ruled that the EU-US Privacy Shield was invalid and invalidated the standard contractual clauses mechanism as previously used, requiring companies to implement additional safeguards for data transfers to third countries like the United States. Facebook was ordered to cease transfers relying solely on SCCs without supplementary measures.
source_rule: We process all of your personal information in the United States, where we maintain our primary data centers.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Microsoft EU Cloud Data Transfer Case
example_background: A group of organizations brought a case against Microsoft, alleging that personal data of EU citizens processed through Azure and Office 365 services was transferred to data centers in the United States without sufficient protection against US surveillance laws. The case, known as the 'Cloud Case,' involved detailed examination of Microsoft's data processing agreements and the effectiveness of standard contractual clauses in light of Schrems II. It focused on the systematic nature of data transfers for cloud services used by public sector and private entities in Europe.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Regional Court of Berlin ruled that transfers relying solely on SCCs were unlawful post-Schrems II, ordering supplementary measures. Microsoft appealed, and the case contributed to the development of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
source_rule: We process all of your personal information in the United States, where we maintain our primary data centers.

process_and_outcome: Authorities in multiple countries issued orders banning or restricting Google Analytics use without additional safeguards, such as data anonymization or EU-based processing. Google updated its tools with IP anonymization and consent mechanisms.
source_rule: We process all of your personal information in the United States, where we maintain our primary data centers.

example_background: Apple updated its privacy policies and practices in response to GDPR requirements starting in 2018. The company implemented tools allowing EU users to access, download, and delete their personal data stored in iCloud, App Store, and other services. Users receive detailed reports on data collected, including location history and advertising profiles. Apple also introduced App Tracking Transparency, giving users control over data sharing across apps.

source_rule: 13.4 Your rightsYou may access, correct, update, or request deletion of your personal information. Please note that we cannot guarantee the factual accuracy of Output from our models. If Output contains factually inaccurate personal information relating to you, you can submit a correction request and we will make reasonable efforts to correct this information — but due to the technical complexity of our models, it may not be feasible for us to do so.

example_name: Meta (Facebook) Irish DPC Right to Erasure Fine
example_background: In 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission investigated Meta's handling of user deletion requests under GDPR. The regulator found that Meta retained certain user data, such as shadow profiles created from non-users' contact uploads, even after account deletion requests. This data included inferred information not directly provided by the user. The investigation stemmed from complaints by privacy advocacy groups about incomplete data erasure.
process_and_outcome: Meta was fined €1.2 billion, the largest GDPR fine to date. The company was ordered to suspend data transfers to the U.S. and ensure proper deletion mechanisms. Meta appealed the decision.
source_rule: 13.4 Your rightsYou may access, correct, update, or request deletion of your personal information. Please note that we cannot guarantee the factual accuracy of Output from our models. example_name: TikTok UK ICO Children's Data Fine
example_background: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office examined TikTok's data practices concerning users under 13. The platform allowed age-inaccurate accounts, retained deleted child data indefinitely, and provided limited access tools for parents or children to review and correct profile information. Biometric data from video analysis was processed without proper safeguards.
process_and_outcome: TikTok received a £12.7 million fine in 2023. The company was required to implement age verification, improve deletion processes, and enhance parental controls.

example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a data breach exposing 400,000 customers' payment card details and login credentials. Affected individuals requested access to breach-related personal data and corrections to any impacted records. BA delayed responses and provided incomplete information on data handling post-breach.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO proposed a £183 million fine, later reduced to £22 million after appeal. BA improved its data subject request portal and security measures.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR TikTok Fine
example_background: In 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission examined TikTok's processing of personal data of minors, including default public account settings and handling of user rights requests. Parents and users submitted objections to processing, restriction requests, and portability demands, which were not always managed in compliance with requirements, particularly regarding age-appropriate processing and data transfers.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €345 million fine following a multi-year investigation involving other EU authorities. TikTok made changes to its privacy settings and rights handling procedures.

example_background: The French CNIL conducted an investigation in 2020 into Amazon France Logistique's use of customer data for targeted advertising. The probe stemmed from complaints about inadequate information on legitimate interest processing and difficulties in objecting or requesting portability of advertising-related data. Automated decision-making and profiling were central to the data uses examined.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon €35 million for non-compliance with GDPR principles related to consent and legitimate interests. Amazon updated its data processing notices and objection mechanisms.

example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR platform implemented automated workflows for handling employee and candidate data subject rights, including objection forms for legitimate interest processing (e.g., recruitment analytics), restriction toggles, and data export tools generating CSV/JSON files for portability, integrated into its cloud services for enterprise clients.
process_and_outcome: SAP underwent GDPR certification and has supported clients in compliance audits without major incidents, positioning it as an example of scalable rights management in HR tech.

example_background: Apple Pay, launched in 2014, uses device-specific tokens instead of actual credit card numbers for transactions. When a user adds a card to Apple Pay, the actual card number is replaced with a unique Device Account Number (token), which is stored securely in the device's Secure Element chip. Payment networks like Visa and Mastercard provision these tokens, ensuring that neither Apple nor merchants receive the underlying card details. This approach was designed to minimize data exposure in mobile payment processing, with extensive testing and certification by payment networks.
process_and_outcome: Apple Pay has operated without major regulatory enforcement for data handling issues. Independent security audits and payment network validations confirm adherence, contributing to widespread adoption and no significant breach incidents related to card data exposure.
source_rule: We have Google Payments, Apple Pay and Stripe conduct payment processing services (respective privacy policies linked). They do not share your credit card information with us.

example_background: PayPal, a major online payment processor, faced allegations in 2023 regarding its handling of customer financial data, including payment information, in violation of consumer protection laws. The case stemmed from unauthorized charges and difficulties in disputing transactions, with claims that PayPal shared or mishandled payment details leading to fraudulent activities. Multiple class action lawsuits highlighted issues in data security and transparency in payment processing.
process_and_outcome: PayPal settled lawsuits for $6.3 million in consumer restitution and fees, plus changes to dispute resolution processes. No admission of wrongdoing, but enhanced monitoring was required.
source_rule: We have Google Payments, Apple Pay and Stripe conduct payment processing services (respective privacy policies linked). They do not share your credit card information with us.

example_background: Square (now Block, Inc.), a payment processing service for small businesses, provides a simplified PCI compliance process through its hardware and software. Merchants using Square's card readers and POS systems complete a reduced Self-Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ A or A-EP) because Square handles all card data without exposing it to the merchant's environment. This tokenization and point-to-point encryption model ensures card details are never stored or transmitted to merchants.
process_and_outcome: Square merchants routinely achieve PCI compliance with minimal effort, validated through annual assessments. No major enforcement actions against Square for payment data handling.
source_rule: We have Google Payments, Apple Pay and Stripe conduct payment processing services (respective privacy policies linked). They do not share your credit card information with us.

example_background: TJX Companies, operator of retail chains like T.J. Maxx, suffered a breach from 2005 to 2007 where hackers accessed Wi-Fi networks at stores to steal 94 million credit and debit card records. The attackers intercepted card data during payment processing at point-of-sale systems, exploiting weak wireless encryption (WEP). TJX outsourced some processing but stored card data on its systems, leading to prolonged exposure.
process_and_outcome: TJX paid over $256 million in settlements, including $24 million to Visa and Mastercard, and faced a $3.5 million FTC settlement. The company upgraded security and faced class actions.
source_rule: We have Google Payments, Apple Pay and Stripe conduct payment processing services (respective privacy policies linked). They do not share your credit card information with us.

example_name: Meta Fined by Irish DPC for Invalid Transfers
example_background: Facebook's European operations, handled by Facebook Ireland Limited, engaged subprocessors in the United States for processing EU users' personal data, including behavioral advertising data. Following the Schrems II ruling in 2020, the Irish Data Protection Commission investigated whether Meta's data transfers complied with GDPR requirements for international transfers. The probe focused on the use of standard contractual clauses with US-based entities without sufficient supplementary measures to counter US surveillance risks. Evidence showed continued reliance on previous transfer mechanisms post-Schrems II, affecting millions of EU users' data flowing to US servers for processing. The case highlighted gaps in risk assessments and implementation of technical measures like encryption or pseudonymization to protect data adequacy.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the Irish DPC imposed a 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta, one of the largest GDPR penalties, for unlawful data transfers over several years. Meta was ordered to cease invalid transfers within five months and bring processing into compliance, with appeals ongoing before the CJEU.
source_rule: We use service providers that may be involved in processing personal information as listed on our subprocessor page. These companies either participate in the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the Swiss-US Data Privacy Framework and the UK extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, which offer adequate protection for European personal information; or we have standard contractual clauses in place with them (including text from modules 1 and 2) which have been approved by the UK, European Commission, and Switzerland.

example_name: TikTok Fined by Dutch and Irish Regulators
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating in the EU through entities in Ireland and the Netherlands, transferred personal data of EU users to China and the US for processing by affiliated companies. The Dutch Data Protection Authority and Irish DPC examined transfers involving subprocessors handling user content, profiles, and analytics data. Concerns arose over the absence of adequate safeguards equivalent to EU standards, particularly given foreign government access risks in recipient countries. Risk assessments were deemed insufficient, and supplementary measures were not implemented despite available mechanisms like SCCs. The investigations covered periods from 2020 onward, impacting young users' data extensively.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the Dutch DPA fined TikTok 35 million euros for unlawful transfers lacking adequate protections. The Irish DPC issued additional findings, contributing to coordinated EU enforcement. TikTok was required to suspend certain transfers and enhance compliance programs.

example_name: Google Analytics Ruled Unlawful by CNIL
example_background: Multiple French websites using Google Analytics transferred EU visitors' IP addresses and user IDs to Google LLC in the US for analytics processing. The French data protection authority, CNIL, received complaints highlighting that these transfers occurred without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II. Google Analytics relies on SCCs, but CNIL found that pseudonymization of IP addresses did not sufficiently mitigate US surveillance risks, as full IP addresses could potentially be accessed. The case involved numerous unnamed websites, with CNIL ordering cessation of transfers after non-compliance with formal notices. Similar actions followed in Austria, Italy, and other EU states.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL issued formal notices and injunctions against websites using Google Analytics without additional measures. Non-compliant sites faced potential fines up to 4% of global turnover. Google adjusted services with IP anonymization options, but regulators maintained transfers remained risky.

example_name: Microsoft Achieves EU-US Data Privacy Framework Certification
example_background: Microsoft Corporation joined the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF) announced in 2023 as a successor to Privacy Shield, committing to annual compliance reviews and transparency reports on US government data requests. Microsoft uses numerous US-based subprocessors for cloud services like Azure and Office 365, processing EU customer data. The company published detailed subprocessor lists and implemented supplementary measures including data localization options and encryption. This framework provides EU adequacy recognition, supplemented by Microsoft's Cloud EU Data Boundary for certain services keeping data within the EU.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft was certified under the DPF in October 2023 by the US Department of Commerce, enabling compliant data transfers without additional SCCs for participating services. Independent audits verified adherence, with no enforcement actions recorded.

Oracle maintains a public subprocessor list, detailing entities like AWS for certain functions, all bound by DPF principles or SCCs with transfer impact assessments. The company offers EU sovereign cloud regions and binding corporate rules for intra-group transfers, ensuring data protection levels match EU standards amid ongoing adequacy debates.
process_and_outcome: Oracle self-certified under DPF in 2023, with US FTC oversight for compliance. No violations reported; Oracle publishes annual transparency reports on data disclosures.

process_and_outcome: Salesforce certified under DPF in 2023; EU regulators have not challenged its mechanisms. Regular audits and customer notifications ensure ongoing compliance.

example_background: AWS provides cloud infrastructure to EU entities, listing US-based subprocessors on its website. This setup allows customers to process personal data compliantly across borders.
process_and_outcome: No regulatory fines; AWS's framework praised in EDPB recommendations. Continuous updates align with evolving guidance from EU authorities.

EU regulators investigated unauthorized transfers of biometric data lacking adequacy decisions, SCCs, or equivalent safeguards, exposing data to US jurisdiction without protections.
process_and_outcome: Multiple fines issued, including 30 million CAD by Dutch DPA in 2024; banned from processing EU data. Ongoing proceedings in several EU countries.

example_name: OpenAI Data Usage Lawsuit by Authors
example_background: In 2023, a group of authors including Sarah Silverman and Richard Kadrey filed a class-action lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging that the company used copyrighted books from internet sources to train its ChatGPT language models without permission or compensation. The plaintiffs claimed that OpenAI scraped vast amounts of data from platforms like Books3 dataset, which contained pirated copies of their works, to develop its generative AI technologies. This practice involved processing millions of books and other texts to fine-tune models like GPT-3 and GPT-4, raising concerns about unauthorized use of personal and intellectual property data for model training purposes. The case highlighted tensions between AI development needs and creators' rights to control how their content is utilized.
process_and_outcome: The lawsuit was filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. OpenAI moved to dismiss parts of the complaint, arguing fair use under copyright law. The court partially denied the motion in 2024, allowing copyright claims to proceed while dismissing some unjust enrichment claims. The case remains ongoing with discovery phases underway.
source_rule: Please note you can object to our use of your information to train our models in your settings. See Consumer FAQs.
involved_parties: Authors (Sarah Silverman, Richard Kadrey, Christopher Golden)

example_name: New York Times Copyright Infringement Suit Against OpenAI and Microsoft
example_background: The New York Times initiated legal action in December 2023 against OpenAI and Microsoft, asserting that the companies incorporated millions of Times articles into the training datasets for ChatGPT and other AI models without authorization. The complaint detailed how AI systems reproduced Times content verbatim in responses, diminishing the value of the publisher's intellectual property. This occurred amid OpenAI's practice of collecting publicly available web data, including journalistic content, to enhance model performance, prompting broader industry discussions on data sourcing ethics.
process_and_outcome: Filed in the Southern District of New York, the case progressed with OpenAI countering that the use constituted fair use. In 2024, the Times released evidence of AI outputs closely mimicking its articles. The litigation continues, with potential implications for AI training practices.

example_name: Meta AI Training Data Scraping Investigations
example_background: European regulators scrutinized Meta's practices in 2024 after the company announced plans to use public European user posts and images from Facebook and Instagram to train its Llama AI models. Meta's policy shift involved automatically opting users into data usage for AI development unless they manually opted out via settings, affecting hundreds of millions of users. This followed similar data harvesting for earlier models, drawing complaints about lack of explicit consent and transparency in processing personal data for commercial AI training.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) opened an investigation in May 2024 into Meta's data use for AI training. Multiple EU complaints were consolidated, with ongoing probes focusing on GDPR compliance. Meta paused some EU training activities pending review.

process_and_outcome: Anthropic publicly documented its opt-out process in FAQs and settings, receiving positive feedback from privacy advocates. No major enforcement actions occurred, and the company continued to expand models like Claude 3 while maintaining the policy, demonstrating sustained adherence.

example_name: Meta €390 Million Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: In 2022, the Irish DPC fined Meta's WhatsApp 225 million euros and Instagram additional amounts totaling around 390 million for GDPR breaches related to processing user data for behavioral advertising. The apps processed data from Facebook interactions for ad targeting without freely given consent or granular opt-outs. Users faced complex interfaces to object to profiling, and data was shared across Meta services without clear separation or easy withdrawal options.
process_and_outcome: After a cross-EU DPA inquiry, fines were imposed for lack of transparency and invalid consent. Meta implemented updated consent banners with one-click opt-outs for personalized ads across its platforms.

example_name: Clearview AI €30.5 Million Fine by Dutch DPA
example_background: In 2022, the Dutch Data Protection Authority fined Clearview AI 30.5 million euros for scraping billions of facial images from the internet without consent and building a database used for law enforcement profiling, which was also sold to private companies for marketing and identification purposes. Individuals had no mechanism to opt out of having their biometric data collected and processed for such uses, violating rights to object to profiling.
process_and_outcome: Following complaints, the Dutch DPA investigated and issued the fine, with other EU authorities taking similar actions. Clearview was ordered to halt operations in the EU and delete data, though it challenged the decision.

example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Meta Platforms, provided messaging services to hundreds of millions of users in Europe. The app collected user data for purposes including targeted advertising, relying on user consent obtained during account setup. The Italian data protection authority investigated user complaints about the inability to effectively withdraw consent for data sharing with Meta's other services. Withdrawal options existed but led to continued data processing under different legal bases without adequate notification, affecting user control over personal information shared across platforms.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante issued a 225 million euro fine in 2021 after finding issues with transparency and consent withdrawal. WhatsApp was required to revise its privacy policy and consent mechanisms. The company complied with some orders while challenging others legally.
source_rule: If we have collected and processed your personal information with your consent, then you can withdraw your consent at any time. Withdrawing your consent will not affect the lawfulness of any processing we conducted prior to your withdrawal, nor will it affect processing of your personal information conducted in reliance on lawful processing grounds other than consent.

example_name: CNIL Enforcement against Google on Post-Mortem Data Access
example_background: In 2021, the French data protection authority investigated Google's handling of deceased users' accounts under its Inactive Account Manager policy. The policy allowed users to designate recipients for data access upon inactivity, presumed death, or explicit confirmation. However, French residents could not fully exercise rights to nominate a third party for data destruction or disclosure after death as outlined in national law. Google's terms did not incorporate these specific mechanisms, leading to incomplete implementation across Europe. The case arose from complaints by relatives of deceased users who faced barriers in accessing or deleting data, highlighting discrepancies between Google's global policy and French requirements for post-mortem data management. The authority noted that Google's approach relied on U.S.-based terms, which overlooked mandatory French provisions for data subjects to appoint trusted third parties via notarial acts or declarations registered with the CNIL.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a formal decision fining Google 150 million euros for multiple GDPR violations, including post-mortem data handling. Google was ordered to update its policies within two months to comply with French law on deceased persons' data rights, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: In France, you can choose to ask us to keep or destroy your personal information, after your death, to a nominated third party. You may also be able to ask us to disclose your personal information, after your death, to a specified recipient.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)
relevant_rules: Article 85 of French Data Protection Act (Law 78-17)
GDPR Article 88 (national specifics for employment, social security, self-employment)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fine for Deceased User Data Practices
example_background: In 2022, Meta's Facebook platform faced scrutiny in France for its procedures on managing accounts of deceased users. Relatives reported difficulties in requesting data deletion or transfer to designated heirs, as Meta's policy primarily offered memorialization or removal upon proof of death but did not integrate French mechanisms for nominating a third party pre-death. The platform processed personal data of millions of French users, and upon death, data remained accessible unless manually reported. CNIL audits revealed that Meta's reliance on U.S. terms of service failed to account for French law requiring data controllers to honor pre-designated third parties for post-mortem data destruction or disclosure. This led to prolonged data retention and unauthorized access by friends or public profiles.
process_and_outcome: Following investigation, CNIL imposed a 90 million euro fine on Meta. The company committed to revising its policies to include French post-mortem rights, with implementation deadlines and periodic compliance reports submitted.
source_rule: In France, you can choose to ask us to keep or destroy your personal information, after your death, to a nominated third party. You may also be able to ask us to disclose your personal information, after your death, to a specified recipient.
relevant_rules: GDPR Recital 27 (processing of personal data of deceased persons)

example_name: OVHcloud Compliance with French Post-Mortem Data Rights
example_background: French cloud provider OVHcloud updated its data processing agreements in 2020 to explicitly support customer nominations of third parties for post-mortem data handling. Customers could register declarations with CNIL or via notarial acts to designate entities for data destruction or disclosure upon death. This was integrated into service contracts for hosting personal data, ensuring that upon notification of death, OVHcloud executed instructions without requiring additional probate processes. The implementation followed CNIL guidelines and was promoted as a best practice for SaaS providers serving French users, addressing the gap where many international firms ignored local inheritance data rules.
process_and_outcome: CNIL reviewed OVHcloud's practices during a 2021 sector audit and confirmed full alignment, issuing no sanctions and citing it positively in guidance documents. OVHcloud continued operations without interruptions.

example_background: In 2020, CNIL examined TikTok's data practices, uncovering prolonged retention of user data post-account deletion and inadequate handling of deceased minors' accounts. example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Data Subject Rights Violations
example_background: Meta Platforms, formerly known as Facebook, faced multiple complaints regarding its handling of data subject rights requests under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Users submitted requests for access to their personal data, deletion of data, and rectification, but the company delayed responses beyond the one-month statutory period, provided incomplete data extracts, and required users to navigate complex processes without clear instructions. This occurred across Europe following the implementation of GDPR in 2018, affecting millions of users who sought to exercise rights through online portals or direct contacts. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) received formal complaints from privacy advocacy groups like NOYB, highlighting systemic issues in Meta's data access tools which often returned partial information or denied requests citing technical difficulties. Internal audits later revealed insufficient resources allocated to rights fulfillment teams, leading to backlogs of over 500,000 pending requests at peak times.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted an investigation starting in 2018, culminating in a 2023 decision fining Meta €1.2 billion for various GDPR infringements including inadequate handling of data subject rights. Meta appealed the decision to the courts, implemented automated tools for faster processing, and agreed to appoint a Data Protection Officer with expanded responsibilities. Additional inquiries by other EU regulators resulted in harmonized enforcement actions.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Breach Response Failure
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber-attack exposing personal data of approximately 400,000 customers, including payment card details and travel information. Following the incident, affected individuals submitted data subject access requests (DSARs) and deletion requests to understand what data was compromised and to have it removed. The airline's customer service channels were overwhelmed, resulting in responses taking up to six months, partial disclosures of breach-related data, and instances where requests were ignored or redirected without resolution. The company's privacy policy directed users to an online portal, but it lacked functionality for complex post-breach inquiries, leading to frustration among passengers who contacted the airline via email and phone as per policy instructions.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and fined British Airways £20 million in 2020 (reduced from £24 million on appeal). The airline enhanced its DSAR processing system with AI-driven automation, reducing average response time to 14 days, and published annual transparency reports on rights requests fulfillment.

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Fines for Rights Handling
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database with personal data of up to 500 million guests. A breach was disclosed in 2018, prompting a surge in data subject rights requests for access, portability, and deletion from affected guests worldwide. Marriott's privacy notice instructed users to submit requests via a dedicated web form or email, but processing delays averaged 3-4 months due to manual reviews and integration issues between legacy systems. Many requesters received automated acknowledgments but no substantive responses, and some were asked for excessive verification before fulfilling requests, contravening GDPR's proportionality principle.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020, with the Dutch DPA adding €725,000 in 2019 for similar issues. Marriott invested in a centralized rights management platform, achieving 95% compliance within one month by 2022, and settled class actions in the US.

example_background: Google faced complaints in 2019-2021 regarding its handling of 'right to be forgotten' requests under GDPR, where European users requested delisting of search results containing their personal data. example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Subject Access Request Fine
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 400,000 customers' personal data, including payment card details and login credentials. Following the breach, numerous individuals submitted data subject access requests (DSARs) under GDPR to understand how their data was handled. The company faced challenges in processing these requests efficiently due to the volume and the need to verify identities amid heightened security concerns. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined the company's overall data protection practices, including its handling of DSARs, and noted delays and inadequacies in responses which impacted transparency obligations. This occurred during a period of regulatory scrutiny on airlines' data management practices post-GDPR implementation.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020, fining British Airways £20 million for infringements including failures in handling DSARs promptly and securely. The airline appealed the decision to the First-tier Tribunal, which in May 2024 reduced the fine to £4.4 million, citing mitigating factors such as cooperation and self-reporting.

example_background: Equifax, one of the major US credit bureaus, was involved in handling consumer disputes and access requests under the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA). Equifax implemented identity verification measures requiring specific documentation like government IDs and utility bills to prevent fraudulent access, particularly citing risks from the breach. Some consumers challenged these requirements as overly burdensome, arguing they delayed access to personal data.

In 2019, Equifax agreed to a settlement including $575 million in consumer redress and $100 million FTC penalty, with mandates to improve dispute handling and identity verification processes without unreasonable delays.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR DSAR Class Action
example_background: Meta Platforms, formerly Facebook, was subject to a class action in the UK brought by Austen Hays on behalf of users who submitted DSARs. Users requested data processed for behavioral advertising, but Meta provided data in an unstructured zipped file format exceeding 1GB, making it difficult to review. Meta required extensive identity verification and did not always explain exemptions applied for legal compliance or claims defense. The case highlighted systemic issues in scalable DSAR fulfillment for large platforms.
process_and_outcome: In April 2023, the UK High Court ruled in favor of Meta, finding that the format provided was compliant with GDPR as it contained all necessary data, though the court noted the usability challenges. No fine was issued, but it set precedents for DSAR delivery standards.

example_name: New York Times v. OpenAI Copyright Infringement
example_background: The New York Times identified that OpenAI's GPT-4 model had been trained on millions of its articles scraped from its website without permission or licensing agreements. Court filings revealed system prompts that instructed the model to use Times content specifically, and verbatim excerpts from paywalled articles were regurgitated in responses. The Times argued this undermined its subscription model and deprived creators of rights over their work used in training large language models. OpenAI's initial stance was that training constituted fair use, but lacked detailed disclosures to affected parties on data sourcing.
process_and_outcome: The New York Times filed a lawsuit in December 2023 in the Southern District of New York seeking injunctions and damages. Discovery processes uncovered internal documents on data scraping. The case remains ongoing as of 2024, with similar suits from other publishers influencing AI training disclosure practices.

example_name: Ticketmaster Data Breach and Rights Handling
example_background: In 2018, a Magecart attack compromised payment data of 40,000 Ticketmaster customers in the UK. Affected non-account holders who purchased tickets via guest checkout struggled to exercise data rights, as initial guidance funneled requests through customer service without a dedicated privacy portal for non-users, leading to delays and complaints to regulators.
process_and_outcome: ICO investigated but issued no fine; Ticketmaster enhanced its privacy contact processes in response to scrutiny.
source_rule: If you are not a user of the Service but wish to exercise any of the above rights to the extent that they may be applicable, you can do so by contacting us at https://xai-privacy.relyance.ai/.
